cataloging_subject.label: Poema Morale (Digby 4 version, with Zupitza's textual study)
cataloging_subject.scope_note: The cataloged intellectual subject is the anonymous late-twelfth-century Poema Morale as represented by Julius Zupitza's complete 1878 article and its principal Digby 4 transcription. CME00086 encodes the entire article, not only the verse: German introduction and witness census on pp. 5–6, the Digby text and apparatus on pp. 6–32, a German stemmatic study on pp. 32–36, and a German dating essay on pp. 36–38. The main verse stream is the Digby copy at ff. 97r–110v; ten stanzas from Trinity B.14.52 occur only as note-scoped supplies at eight Digby lacunae. The record keeps Zupitza's editorial prose, the other manuscript witnesses and prior printed editions, the U-M encoding, and the repository XML as distinct layers rather than assigning their dates, languages or responsibilities to the medieval work.

catalog_summary.title: Poema Morale (Digby 4 version, with Zupitza's textual study)
catalog_summary.author: Anonymous
catalog_summary.date: Poema Morale: late twelfth century; Digby 4 copy: early thirteenth century; first complete Digby edition: 1878
catalog_summary.region: England; original tentatively localized to the southern South-East Midlands around Middlesex or London; Digby copy described as Kentish

titles.value: Poema Morale (Digby 4 version, with Zupitza's textual study)

assertion.notes: The qualifier distinguishes this complete article-level representation from the abstract work and from other witness versions; it is not a manuscript title.

scope.description: Printed title of Zupitza's article and inherited U-M item title

notes: This is the conventional scholarly title, not an authorial or manuscript title.

assertion.notes: Betty Hill proposed this descriptive title in 1977; Poema Morale remains the conventional DIMEV title.

titles.value: Ic am elder, þanne ic wes, a wintre and ec a lore
Crist ȝeue us lede swich lif and habbe swichne ende, þet we moten þider cumen, þanne we hennes wende

scope.description: Direct transcription of Digby 4, ff. 97r–110v, apart from explicitly stated editorial normalization and correction
Comparison of six witness streams through the cited Furnivall and Morris editions; direct physical consultation is established only for Digby 4
Authorship of the German witness census, stemmatic argument and dating essay

date_statements.display: Late twelfth century; Hill proposed 1154–1189, while Zupitza argued approximately 1170

assertion.notes: This dates the abstract work, not the Digby copy. The normalized range follows Hill's historical proposal; Zupitza's c. 1170 falls within it.

date_statements.display: Early thirteenth century in Oxford, Bodleian Library MS. Digby 4

assertion.notes: This is the first known physical attestation of the cataloged Digby version, not a claim that Digby is the earliest witness of Poema Morale across the tradition.

date_statements.display: First complete Digby 4 transcription published by Julius Zupitza in 1878

assertion.notes: Hickes had printed thirty-seven selected Digby stanzas in 1705; 1878 is the first complete publication of this witness version, not the first publication of Poema Morale from any witness.

places.label: Southern South-East Midlands (Middlesex or London area)
notes: Hill's reconstruction concerns the original work, not the production or dialect of Digby 4.

places.label: Kent
notes: Zupitza's language-based classification of the Digby copy; it is not proof of the work's composition place.
This broad origin does not identify an exact composition site.

assertion.notes: Hill reconstructs the original version on the southern border of the South-East Midland dialect area around Middlesex or London; this is a critical localization, not documentary proof.
Zupitza assigns the scribe's linguistic forms to Kent. The claim belongs to this witness copy and is not transferred to the original work.

language_statements.label: Middle English
notes: German editorial prose is substantive in the encoded article but belongs to the edition layer and is therefore described at part level rather than exposed as a work-language facet.

form_statements.description: The cataloged medieval work is verse. CME00086 also retains extensive German editorial prose in three framing parts, but that prose belongs to Zupitza's edition rather than the anonymous work's form.

assertion.notes: Hill calls the work a verse-sermon; verse homily is the normalized corpus label.

subject_statements.label: Death, salvation, and the Last Judgment
Almsgiving and care for the poor
Philology and textual criticism
notes: This describes substantive editorial-study parts retained by CME00086; it is not presented as a subject of the anonymous homily itself.

summaries.value: An aging speaker regrets a life wasted in sin and turns preacher, urging repentance, charity, almsgiving, detachment from transient wealth, and preparation for death and judgment. The poem contrasts the pains of hell with the unending joy of heaven and closes with a prayer to live and die well. CME00086 frames Zupitza's Digby 4 transcription with a German census of six witness streams, a detailed stemmatic argument, and a dating essay that rejects a tenth- or eleventh-century origin and proposes composition around 1170.

Four encoded sections retain the whole 1878 article: witness introduction (pp. 5–6); Digby text and apparatus (pp. 6–32); manuscript-relationship study and stemma (pp. 32–36); and dating essay (pp. 36–38). The Digby text contains 191 numbered four-short-line groups. Ten additional T groups at eight Digby lacunae are embedded in notes, and four verse lines are quoted in the stemmatic discussion.

The XML covers every printed page from 5 through 38 and retains Zupitza's prose, edited verse, notes, page breaks and Digby folio milestones. It does not encode an image or branch structure for the graphical stemma on p. 36, only the description 'family tree of manuscripts.' The full-volume scan is therefore required to inspect that diagram.

The main text is a witness-specific Digby version rather than a reconstructed critical text. Zupitza's T supplies are separately scoped note passages, and E/e/J/L/T remain comparison witnesses mediated by cited earlier print editions. The German article prose is described as editorial framing and is not promoted to the anonymous work's language or form facets.

extent.description: Encoding metrics for the complete article: one DIV1; four DIV2; 201 LG and 808 L in total. Section I contains 191 direct numbered Digby LG / 764 direct short-line L plus ten nested T-supply LG / forty L in notes. Section II adds four quoted L. These counts describe XML representation and apparatus, not the abstract work's DIMEV extent of 398 long lines in rhyming couplets. The source prints the Digby copy as 191 groups of four short lines, equivalent to 382 paired long lines if mechanically recombined.

content_parts.label: Witness introduction and source census
content_parts.summary: German introduction stating three aims and identifying D, E, e, J, L and T, their loci, selected dates, physical features and prior editions.
notes: German is an edition-layer language and is not a whole-work language facet.

content_parts.label: I. Das Poema Morale nach D
content_parts.summary: The anonymous Digby 4 version of Poema Morale, printed as 191 numbered groups of four short lines with German editorial apparatus and ten Trinity-supplied stanzas embedded at eight lacunae.
notes: The medieval work itself is verse; this encoded part is marked mixed only because it substantively includes Zupitza's prose editorial declaration and notes.
The main Digby stream is 191 LG / 764 L. Ten T-supply LG / forty L are note descendants and are not counted as direct Digby lines.
The printed and encoded stanza after 155 is labeled 165, a source error for 156; the sequence then resumes 157 and later has the genuine 165.

content_parts.label: II. Verhältniss der Hss. des Poema Morale zu einander
content_parts.summary: German textual analysis dividing the six witness streams into proposed groups, testing dependency through shared and divergent readings, and concluding with a graphical stemma.
notes: Four L elements encode a quoted two-line reading pair; they are apparatus quotation, not an intrinsic new poem section.
The printed p. 36 stemma is present only as FIGURE/FIGDESC in XML, so this part is structurally partial at the visual layer.

content_parts.label: III. Alter des Poema Morale
content_parts.summary: German dating essay rejecting Morris's proposed tenth- or early-eleventh-century source on lexical and rhyme grounds and proposing composition around 1170.
notes: Zupitza's c. 1170 conclusion is a historical scholarly claim; modern work metadata retains Hill's broader late-twelfth-century analysis.

evidence.citation: General Corpus pinned CME source XML, CME00086.xml.
evidence.locator: BODY/DIV1 and four DIV2; 191 direct poem LG; PB 5–38; MILESTONE 97r–110v; TEMPHEAD
evidence.summary: Establishes exact article structure, Middle English and German content, titles, verse text and apparatus, page and folio boundaries, editorial signature, typographic markup and stemma placeholder.

evidence.citation: Element, hierarchy and content audit of repository CME00086.xml against the complete 1878 scan.
evidence.locator: Counts under BODY/DIV1 and each DIV2; first and last text; nested note LGs; p. 36 figure; stanza sequence around 155
evidence.summary: Counts one DIV1, four DIV2, 201 LG, 808 L, 79 NOTE, 34 PB, 28 MILESTONE and 835 italic HI. Separates 191 direct Digby LG / 764 L from ten nested T-supply LG / forty L and four stemmatic-quotation L; confirms complete p. 5–38 coverage, source-level 165-for-156 numbering, and FIGDESC-only loss of the stemma geometry.

evidence.locator: printed pp. 5–38 / PDF pages 17–50; Digby text pp. 6–32; stemma p. 36
evidence.summary: Primary evidence for title, editor, source witness and locus, six-witness census, editorial practices, ten T supplies, four-part article structure, historical stemma and proposed c. 1170 date.

evidence.citation: Digital Index of Middle English Verse, version 1.0, DIMEV/NIMEV 1272, record-2113.
evidence.locator: Records.xml record-2113 / wit-2113-1 through wit-2113-7; Manuscripts.xml; Bibliography.xml Zupitza1878a
evidence.summary: Versioned scholarly evidence for preferred title, incipit, 398-line couplet extent in the fullest tradition, moral-advice subject, seven witness entries, exact loci and Zupitza edition listing.

evidence.citation: Betty Hill, 'The Twelfth-Century Conduct of Life, Formerly the Poema Morale or A Moral Ode,' Leeds Studies in English, new series 9 (1977): 97–144.
evidence.locator: witness census pp. 97–99; date and localization pp. 109–115; metre, genre and content pp. 115–144
evidence.summary: Modern scholarly evidence for anonymous authorship, title history, seven complete witness versions, late-twelfth-century composition, tentative South-East Midlands localization, verse-sermon genre, metre and religious-didactic content.

evidence.citation: General Corpus lineage manifest for CME00086: audited article, prior editions, witness access and whole-manuscript facsimile mappings.
evidence.locator: relations, editorial_practices, access, rights and evidence
evidence.summary: Supports the exact direct-versus-mediated source chain, T supply boundaries, full-codex cache mappings, figure loss, rights distinctions and unresolved complete-Digby acquisition route.

open_questions.question: Can Poema Morale's composition be dated or localized more securely than late-twelfth-century composition and Hill's tentative southern South-East Midlands reconstruction around Middlesex or London?
next_steps: Review post-1977 metrical, dialectological and manuscript scholarship.
Keep original composition, witness language and copying place separate.

open_questions.question: Which parts of Zupitza's 1878 U–Y/Z–X/W stemma remain accepted after Hill and later witness scholarship?
next_steps: Compare the full readings in diplomatic editions and modern stemmatic studies.
Do not promote Zupitza's hypothetical nodes to physical witnesses or current consensus.

open_questions.question: Will Bodleian Imaging Services supply a complete color facsimile of the whole 112-leaf Digby 4 codex, and under what private-storage and republication terms?
next_steps: Request all boards, spine, pastedowns, flyleaves, blanks, leaves and edges where offered, not only ff. 97r–110v.
Map the work portion separately and obtain explicit Git, scholarly-publication and commercial print-on-demand terms.

open_questions.question: Should reader-facing output annotate the source's first stanza 165 as 156 while preserving the diplomatic value?
next_steps: Preserve the source numbering in diplomatic output.
Add a transparent correction note only if the rendering model can distinguish source and normalized labels.

notes: Poema Morale is anonymous; Julius Zupitza is editor, direct Digby transcriber, collator and author of the German study, not author of the medieval work.
The source edition date 1878 is kept separate from the work's late-twelfth-century composition and the early-thirteenth-century Digby copy.
The Digby copy's Kentish classification is witness-level. It does not supersede Hill's distinct and tentative reconstruction of the original work's dialect region.

DIMEV's 398 lines describe the abstract work in its fullest long-line/couplet form. CME's 764 direct Digby L elements are short-line encoding units; ten T-supply groups and four quoted lines are apparatus.
The complete article is textually retained, but content_structure_status is partial because the p. 36 graphical stemma has been reduced to a generic figure description without its branch geometry.
German prose and Latin conventional title strings are recorded in titles and part descriptions but are not promoted to work-level language facets.
Whole-manuscript facsimiles for Jesus 29 and Trinity B.14.52 are supporting comparison sources, not the source of the continuous Digby text.
No public Digby 4 facsimile was inferred from the existence of a catalog or generic Digital Bodleian collection page.
